food_subgroup: Thai coconut milk and galangal chicken soup; lighter than Thai curries — 150ml coconut milk per bowl versus ~200ml in curries

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Tom Kha Gai specifically. Closest parent concept: coconut milk soup with chicken (FOODON:03315595 — chicken soup, closest parent only). Gap noted; no code invented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thai coconut milk galangal chicken soup — closest parent match in FoodEx2 hierarchy; no exact code exists for Tom Kha Gai
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RCD is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a coconut-milk-based Asian soup with chicken. Exact Tom Kha Gai code does not exist in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Tom Kha Gai or Thai coconut galangal soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Tom Kha Gai is approximately 350–355ml (355g), comprising 150ml full-fat coconut milk, 200ml chicken broth, 80g sliced chicken breast, and aromatics including galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, straw mushrooms, chilli, lime juice, and fish sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Tom Kha Gai — Thailand INMU Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Institute of Nutrition, Mahidol University (INMU), Thailand Food Composition Tables; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for component cross-validation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables provide direct compositional data for Tom Kha Gai. Values per 100g are derived from the INMU record for this dish. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for cross-validation of coconut milk and chicken breast component values. INMU is an FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional database and qualifies as an approved primary source under the FAO/INFOODS SEA category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index estimation for Tom Kha Gai — analogous estimate based on coconut milk soup with chicken and low-digestible-carbohydrate profile
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); peer-reviewed GI literature for coconut milk and low-carbohydrate soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Kha Gai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~25 is estimated based on: (1) the dominant macronutrient profile — high fat from coconut milk (lauric acid C12:0 dominant), moderate protein from chicken breast, and very low digestible carbohydrate (2.3g/100g, ~8g per bowl); (2) analogous low-carbohydrate coconut milk soups in the University of Sydney GI Database which cluster in the GI 20–30 range; (3) the fat-dominant matrix significantly attenuating postprandial glucose response. GL per 355g bowl = (25 × 8.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~2.1, rounded to 1.5 per INMU carbohydrate figure. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from fish sauce — approximately 700mg per 355g bowl (197mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Tom Kha Gai contributes approximately 700mg sodium per bowl, predominantly from fish sauce; individuals with hypertension should treat this as a significant sodium contribution within a meal and limit additional high-sodium dishes in the same sitting, targeting total meal sodium below 1500mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (GL ~1.5–2.1 per bowl) due to high fat and protein content with minimal digestible carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tom Kha Gai is one of the most glycaemically favourable Thai restaurant dishes for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes — the high coconut milk fat content slows gastric emptying, the chicken breast provides satiety-promoting protein, and the total digestible carbohydrate per bowl is approximately 8g, yielding a GL of ~1.5–2.1; it can be consumed freely as a starter without significant postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk — approximately 14.9g saturated fat per 355g bowl (4.2g/100g), predominantly lauric acid (C12:0)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each bowl of Tom Kha Gai provides approximately 15g saturated fat from coconut milk (lauric acid C12:0 dominant), which represents 75% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit for cardiovascular disease management; individuals with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should consume this dish occasionally and balance it with lower-saturated-fat choices across the day, noting that lauric acid raises both LDL and HDL cholesterol and its net cardiovascular effect remains debated in current literature.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Galangal (galangin) and lemongrass (citral, geraniol) with mild in vitro anti-inflammatory activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tom Kha Gai contains galangal (Alpinia galanga), whose active compound galangin has demonstrated mild anti-inflammatory activity in vitro, and lemongrass, which contains citral and geraniol with in vitro anti-inflammatory effects; while clinical evidence in rheumatoid arthritis is limited, the dish is not contraindicated and the moderate saturated fat content is the primary consideration for systemic inflammation management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Kha Gai (ต้มข่าไก่) is a classic Thai coconut galangal chicken soup originating from Central Thai cuisine. The name translates literally as 'galangal boil chicken' — kha (ข่า) meaning galangal is the defining aromatic ingredient that distinguishes this soup from Tom Yum Goong. A standard restaurant bowl (~350ml, 355g) is prepared by simmering 150ml full-fat coconut milk with 200ml chicken broth, 80g sliced chicken breast, galangal slices, one lemongrass stalk, kaffir lime leaves, 50g straw mushrooms, fresh chilli, lime juice, and fish sauce. The result is a lush, creamy, mildly spiced soup with a warm piney-camphor fragrance from galangal, gentle citrus from kaffir lime, and a rich coconut milk base. Nutritionally per 355g bowl: approximately 281 kcal, 18g protein, 18g fat (of which ~15g saturated fat from coconut milk lauric acid C12:0), 8g carbohydrate, 700mg sodium. GI ~25 (low), GL ~1.5–2.1 (very low) — making it one of the most glycaemically favourable Thai restaurant dishes. Tom Kha is lighter than Thai curries (150ml vs ~200ml coconut milk per serving). Galangal is NOT interchangeable with ginger — it has a distinctly piney, camphor-like flavour profile and contains the anti-inflammatory compound galangin. Straw mushrooms contribute trace Vitamin D and dietary fibre. Source: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables (FAO/INFOODS SEA affiliate). Confidence: HIGH. Commonly served at Thai restaurants in Singapore including Nakhon Kitchen and Jai Thai.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values sourced directly from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, an FAO/INFOODS SEA-affiliated regional database with direct compositional data for Tom Kha Gai. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology with documented basis. Confidence 1.0 on nutrient values per input record.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Tom Kha Gai are not separately reported in INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy consulted for cross-validation but does not provide monounsaturated fat breakdown for this composite dish. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g — INMU does not report polyunsaturated fatty acid fractions for Tom Kha Gai, and no approved source provides this breakdown for the composite dish as prepared. water_g set to -1: moisture content for Tom Kha Gai as a composite soup dish is not separately tabulated in INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per INMU source for this traditional preparation containing no hydrogenated fats. All three core macros (protein_g 5.1, fat_g 5.1, carbohydrate_g 2.3) are non-zero and well-supported. GI ~25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database methodology for low-carbohydrate coconut milk soups; no direct GI measurement exists for Tom Kha Gai. GL per bowl calculated as (25 × 8.2g available carbohydrate per 355g serving) / 100 ≈ 2.1; input record states 1.5 based on INMU carbohydrate figure — both values documented. Sodium per bowl ~700mg (197mg/100g × 3.55). Dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. Halal status: traditional preparation uses fish sauce (no pork); halal-certified fish sauce variants are widely available in Singapore Thai restaurants.
